title: Caruana vs Carlsen — 2018 WCC, Game 12
description: In the final classical game Carlsen outplays Caruana in a Sveshnikov and reaches a clearly better position, yet famously offers a draw to head straight for the tiebreaks. Annotated by GM Sam Shankland.

conceptChecks.prompt: Carlsen plays 8...Ne7 ("!?"). How does the annotator characterize it?

options.text: It's the first surprise of the game — a deviation from the 8...Nb8 seen in Games 8 and 10.
options.explanation: Correct. The note: "The first surprise. Games 8 and 10 saw 8...Nb8."

options.text: It's Carlsen's habitual main line that Caruana was surely booked against.
options.explanation: No — the annotator calls it 'the first surprise,' a change from his earlier 8...Nb8.
The annotator makes no such claim; it's a repositioning surprise, not a tactic.

conceptChecks.hint: Compare it to what Black played in the earlier games of the match.

conceptChecks.prompt: What is the idea behind Caruana's 12.h4 ("!?")?

options.text: White plays against the g6-knight; the threat of h4-h5 is annoying for Black to meet.
options.explanation: Correct. The note: "White aims to play against the g6 knight. The threat of h4-h5 is not particularly subtle, but a bit annoying for Black to meet."

options.text: It simply prepares quick kingside castling.
options.explanation: No — White's idea is the h5 thrust against the knight, not castling.

options.text: It defends against a Black mating attack on h2.
options.explanation: The annotator describes h4 as an aggressive plan targeting g6, not a defensive move.

conceptChecks.hint: Which black piece does the h-pawn want to harass?

conceptChecks.prompt: The annotator marks 18.f3 with a "?!". Why does he criticize it?

options.text: It's too defensive and too slow; the natural 18.Be2 was called for instead.
options.explanation: Correct. The note: "The first dubious move of the game... this strikes me as too defensive and too slow," and he gives 18.Be2 as the move that "looks by far the most natural."

options.text: It is forced in order to avoid losing a piece.
options.explanation: No — the annotator offers 18.Be2 as a better, natural alternative, so f3 was not forced.
No such claim; he calls f3 dubious and passive, not a material grab.

conceptChecks.hint: Was this a fighting move or a timid one?

conceptChecks.prompt: Carlsen plays 22...Bg6 ("!"). What does the annotator say about this moment?

options.text: It prepares ...f5 to roll the central majority; the timing exploits that White can't reply g3-g4 because of the hanging c-pawn.
options.explanation: Correct. The note: "f7-f5 is coming... I think Black is already seriously better. Note that Carlsen chose this moment... since White cannot comfortably play g3-g4 in response due to the hanging c-pawn."

options.text: It is a defensive move to hold a worse position.
options.explanation: The opposite — the annotator says Black is 'already seriously better' and pressing.

options.text: It forces an immediate draw by repetition.
options.explanation: No; Black is playing for the win with his central pawns.

conceptChecks.hint: Why is this the right moment, and what does it prepare?

conceptChecks.prompt: The annotator gives 25...a5 a "!?". What is his verdict?

options.text: It isn't the most precise — 25...exf4 or 25...b5 were stronger — but Black should still be winning.
options.explanation: Correct. The note: "This move is not best. But it doesn't really bother me- I think Black should still win," while offering 25...exf4 and 25...b5 as alternatives.

options.text: It's an outright blunder that throws away the win.
options.explanation: No — the annotator says it 'doesn't really bother me' and that Black should still win.

options.text: It's clearly the best move in the position.
options.explanation: The annotator explicitly says it 'is not best,' preferring exf4 or b5.

conceptChecks.hint: Inaccurate, but does it actually spoil anything?

conceptChecks.prompt: The annotator slaps 29...a4 with "??". Why is it such a serious error?

options.text: It opens the b4-square for White's queen and denies Black's own pieces the a4-square, sabotaging the only winning plan of ...b7-b5; 29...Ba4! was the convincing route.
options.explanation: Correct. The note: "His only plan to win the game is b7-b5... now he has cleared the b4-square for White's queen!... the pawn on a4 deprives Black's knight and bishop access to this square." He gives 29...Ba4! as "the most convincing route to victory."

options.text: It simply hangs the a-pawn for nothing.
options.explanation: That's not the point — the flaw is strategic: it frees Qb4 and kills the ...b5 break.

options.text: It walks into a forced checkmate.
options.explanation: No mate is mentioned; the criticism is that it ruins Black's winning plan.

conceptChecks.hint: What square does the pawn surrender, and which plan does it wreck?
